title: Resolving Type Inference in a Generic Math Calculator

statement.inputFormat: A string representing the operation to perform (e.g. 'add', 'subtract', 'multiply', 'divide')
statement.outputFormat: The result of the operation (e.g. a number or a string)
statement.notes: The calculator should correctly handle string arguments and return the result as a string.

examples.explanation: The calculator correctly adds 1 to the input number 5.
The calculator correctly subtracts 1 from the input number 5.
The calculator correctly multiplies the input number 5 by 2.
The calculator correctly divides the input number 5 by 2.
The calculator correctly adds 1 to the input string 'a'.

hints.text: Pay attention to the type inference for the calculator methods.

editorial.title: Correct Type Inference for the Math Calculator
editorial.intuition: The type inference for the calculator methods should be corrected to handle string arguments correctly.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would be to manually specify the types for each method, but this would be cumbersome and error-prone.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use generics to define the type of the calculator methods.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution would be to use a single generic type parameter for the calculator class and define the methods accordingly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness would involve showing that the calculator methods correctly handle string arguments and return the result as a string.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the calculator with the corrected type inference would show that it correctly handles string arguments and returns the result as a string.
